scenarioName: Burn with Inhalation Injury — Preemptive RSI

scenarioStory.history: 50-year-old male pulled from a one-story house fire by an engine crew after being trapped in a back bedroom for an estimated 8-10 minutes. He was found prone on the floor, conscious. Now complaining of a sore throat and difficulty swallowing. Voice quality has been worsening since extraction.
scenarioStory.discussion: Identify sentinel signs of airway burn and the rationale for preemptive intubation. Apply the MATC Burns protocol including TBSA estimation and Rule of 10 fluid resuscitation. Recognize that SpO2 is unreliable in CO poisoning. Manage concurrent thermal, inhalation, and chemical (CO/CN) injuries. Coordinate transport destination based on burn severity and airway involvement
scenarioStory.course: Arrival — Conscious with Inhalation Injury Signs: Crew arrives to a conscious, talking patient with multiple sentinel signs of airway burn: carbonaceous sputum, hoarse voice, singed nasal hairs, and developing stridor. He is currently maintaining his own airway but the trajectory is concerning. Pulse oximetry reads 96% which may be falsely reassuring given probable CO exposure. → Preemptive RSI Before Airway Closes: Voice is now markedly more hoarse. Stridor is continuous at rest. The window for easy intubation is closing. Crew commits to RSI now, while the patient is still cooperative and the airway anatomy is still recognizable. → Post-Intubation and Burn Resuscitation: Tube confirmed. Crew initiates fluid resuscitation per Rule of 10, continues 100% oxygen for CO exposure, and arranges transport to the most appropriate burn/trauma center with ED notification.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Burns and smoke inhalation after structure fire rescue

scenarioEvents.name: Arrival — Conscious with Inhalation Injury Signs
scenarioEvents.description: Crew arrives to a conscious, talking patient with multiple sentinel signs of airway burn: carbonaceous sputum, hoarse voice, singed nasal hairs, and developing stridor. He is currently maintaining his own airway but the trajectory is concerning. Pulse oximetry reads 96% which may be falsely reassuring given probable CO exposure.

scenarioEvents.name: Preemptive RSI Before Airway Closes
scenarioEvents.description: Voice is now markedly more hoarse. Stridor is continuous at rest. The window for easy intubation is closing. Crew commits to RSI now, while the patient is still cooperative and the airway anatomy is still recognizable.
relatedChecklist: Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography

scenarioEvents.name: Post-Intubation and Burn Resuscitation
scenarioEvents.description: Tube confirmed. Crew initiates fluid resuscitation per Rule of 10, continues 100% oxygen for CO exposure, and arranges transport to the most appropriate burn/trauma center with ED notification.

checklist.title: Recognize sentinel signs of airway burn and commit to preemptive RSI
100% oxygen via NRB to treat presumed CO toxicity (SpO2 unreliable)
Establish two large-bore IVs avoiding burned skin
Perform RSI with Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg + Rocuronium 1 mg/kg, using smaller ETT with bougie
Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography
Initiate Rule of 10 LR fluid resuscitation (200 mL/hr for this patient)
Cover burns with dry sterile dressings and prevent hypothermia
Transport to burn center with early notification
Fentanyl for pain management during preparation
Monitor for cyanide toxicity in an enclosed-space fire victim
